title: 2025/12/17 - SSC (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2025-12-17 - Social Services and Community Committee - Social Services and Community [Part 1]
description: Wednesday 17 December | Social Services and Community Briefing on New Zealand's ageing population Hearing of evidence: Retirement Commissioner Jane Wrightson, Retirement Commissioner Michelle Reyers, Policy Lead Dr Paul Spoonley
descriptionSummary: Social Services and Community. Briefing on New Zealand's ageing population. Retirement Commissioner

chunks.text: Are you?
Morning.

Yeah, please. Thank you. Well, good morning and welcome to the Social Services Community League Committee. Uh, thank you for coming in this morning, bright and new on uh, what's gonna be the pumps last thing day today. So good. Pardon? An important, an important topic. Um, got a briefing from you on population this morning. We got to 30 minutes, so look, um, in your hands and take it away.
Alright, well,

uh, I'm Jane Wrightson, the retirement commissioner, and this is my colleague, Dr. Michelle Reyes, the policy lead at the commission. Um, thank you for inviting us to brief you this morning on the long term outlet for New Zealand's population. Um, at this time of year. I certainly feel I'm aging fast, and I'm sure you do too. Indeed, We are for this year.

So let's talk about how it's, what it's like for all of us, shall we? Um, you, as you probably know, the role of the commission is to provide independent, uh, evidence-based advice to ensure the retirement income system remains fair, sustainable, and trusted now and in the decades ahead. Um, and the first message is that we are starting actually from a strong position.

Our retirement savings system, uh, which includes New Zealand Super Kiwi Saver and other private savings, performs well by international standards. It provides broad coverage, has relatively low fiscal costs and benefits from a younger population than many other countries. But as you know, the world in the policy context is changing.

Um, historically, new Zealand's performed well in reducing poverty amongst people age 65 and over. But this is changing. Uh, financial vulnerability is increasing for over 60 fives. 44% of older New Zealanders feel exposed or poor. The poverty rate for 65 plus has risen to 16.8%, which is above the OECD average of 14.2.

Um, we know that housing costs for the 65 plus are increasing. Uh, mortgage free ownership, uh, fell from 83% to 71%, and 13% are still paying mortgages with 13% renting. Um, as KiwiSaver continues to grow in importance within the system, existing disparities will probably continue to widen, um, as retirement savings are in increments by factors such as lifetime earnings and employment type.

Just so you know, men's kiwi saver balances are on average 25% higher than women's, uh, and only 44% of, uh, the self employee contribute to Kiwi Saver and they contribute half as much as, uh, overall as employees. So I think it's important I point this out as these truths from our data differ somewhat from the more common stereotypes of older New Zealanders and the supposed great wealth transfer.

In short, the wealth is only transferred to a few and there are a few more problems emerging. So what's happening next, which I think is what this committee is going to be wrestling with. So we've, we've thought hard about the demographic trends over the next 50 years. Um, and as you clearly know, New Zealand, um, is experiencing a significant demographic shift.

The proportion of people aged over 65 and over is increasing rapidly driven by longer life expectancies and declining birth rates. By 2050, the number of people age 65 plus is projected to increase by about 50%, while the number of people under 40 will remain roughly the same as it is today.

This is not a temporary baby boomer effect. It is a sustained trend due to sustained changes in birth rates and longevity. So what are the implications for an aging population for retirement and social services? In short, one word, profound NZ super is projected to remain a significant component of retirement income, but the fiscal cost will, on a gross basis, rise from 54%, sorry, 5.4% of GDP today to 6.6%, uh, by 2050.

It is important to note that this is still below the OECD average of 10.2%. So we are still kind of in the middle. Um, more people will be living longer in retirement and the diversity of older New Zealand municipal increase with greater variation in health, housing, and financial circumstances, uh, and home ownership amongst older people is declining.

And more people will enter the pension years with mortgages or as renters, which increases the cost of financial stress and material hardship. Also to note, NZ super isn't designed to cover significant housing costs. You meant to be mortgage free or being affordable public housing by that point. So there are challenges and opportunities, uh, facing the government of today and governments of the future.

The system simplicity and universality of strengths, but not all groups benefit equally, women, Maori Pacific, peoples renters and the self-employed face greater risk of hardship in in retirement wealth and income inequality amongst older people are likely to widen unless policy settings adapt and the demand for age appropriate housing, accessible healthcare and the and community support will grow.

Um, every three years, a review is carried out by the retirement commission. This is your holiday reading. Um, as required by law, it provides the government with independent advice on how retirement policies are performing and what changes might be needed. The review of retirement income policies 2025 was published only last month, and it offers, uh, an opportunity to take a system-wide view.

It looks across NZ super KiwiSaver and private savings to understand how different parts of the system interact. So this joined up approach helps identify gaps, overlaps, and opportunities for improvement. This year's review ta uh, includes targeted changes we can make now such as improving KiwiSaver settings and extending support through parental leave.

It also proposes reforms to strengthen long term stewardship and optimize areas for future thinking. The, the most critical recommendation from the review is to strengthen long term stewardship of our retirement system. We are proposing a 10 year retirement income roadmap guided by a a, a cross party parliamentary working group, and a Pan sector stewardship group led by the retirement commission.

This approach is designed to most important, build enduring political consensus and stability across electoral cycles to ensure that policy is joined up across NZ super KiwiSaver and private savings and to enable regular monitoring, public engagement.

Most important and evidence-based adjustment as our population and economy change, the worst thing that can happen is piecemeal announcements that don't look properly at the long term and don't consider social impacts equity issues and the need for long-term planning.

If you want to fiddle with New Zealand super, for instance, then for heaven's sake, look at how to make kiwi save better in the longer term, but the right incentives for the right people. At the same time. It's a system. I often frame this as a women's issue as much as anything because women have fewer retirement savings.

We live longer, and we disproportionately affected by economic life shocks like divorce or business failure. This is not just about fiscal issues, it's about the kind of society we want to live in and how we want to treat our older people. So our work at the commission in, in, in conclusion is guided by belief that retirement income policies must be evidence-based and shaped by long-term thinking.

The issues we face are not going away, and it's crucial that our politicians work together on enduring solutions. We have time, this is a chance to pause, to take stock, and to commit to a practical roadmap to the future, one that extends beyond political cycles and focuses on stability, dignity, and mana in retirement.

So this is really important because we can ensure that New Zealanders not only have security in retirement today, but that future generations, the ones paying for current pensioners, can also look forward to a system that is fair, sustainable, and trusted.

Um, I'm grateful that you are digging deep into these issues because it needs to be done. Uh, and we are here now to answer questions. Ana, Uh, thank you very much for that. Um, uh, very, very interesting, uh, and a whole lot of issues there. But I I might just kick off one question. Um, I've got a whole a lot, but I'll have to spread around the table. I hope We can answer them.

Just, um, the, your, your, your points that you made around, uh, the superannuation scheme not being designed for significant housing costs on one hand, and in the other hand you said the 44% or only 44% of self-employed, um, have KiwiSaver and of them, half of them, uh, well are only contributing to half the rate of an employed person on a on average.

I, I understand. What, what are your, what are your thoughts about what the potential implications are there? Because we, we are pretty significant self-employed cohort in this Country. That's right. We do, we are doing some very deep data digging at the moment. I've got somebody buried in the bowels of the, um, IDI, the, the big, the big data lake to try and find out what we know, because what we know isn't much, uh, we know that ke uh, that, um, self-employed people are underrepresented at the KiwiSaver scheme for a, for a variety of reasons.

What we don't know is whether they're providing for their retirement in other ways, or whether they're simply relying on selling their business for retirement, which would be a, you know, the, the one shot, um, goal, which is always a problem. Um, so we don't know enough, but we don't think, certainly the kiwi safer system is not designed particularly well for self-employed people.

There's no, no question of that. So, supplementary on that, are you also looking at, um, how many of them are using a rental property, you know, as a, as a, as a retirement, um, bank Plan? Yeah. Yeah, I think so. That's, that's covered on the d isn't it? That's why this is the stuff that we don't know what we know, we know quite a lot now about what's happening in Kiwi Save not enough, but a bit.

But what we don't know is, is how people if, if, and how people are saving outside of Kiwi state for retirement. And that data is very hard to come by, because oddly enough, the government does know everything about your fiscal life. So It's, I've got us up on this. I just, um, it's, I'm, I'm like, Joseph, I've got so many questions I'd like to ask you, and it, your work is so important.

Um, but in terms of these, um, self-employed people, how much do we know about the levels of actual income that they have? Because I mean, I'm really interested in that work by Elizabeth Golden about greedy jobs versus the women often are in low paid work and top up because there needs to be flexibility in a family, and it means they're often self-employed, but I think it masks a lot of self-employed masks, underemployment, and really low amounts of income.

And so I wanna know, it might just be unrealistic to think that those people are saving anything. Like I've known people in that situation who've saved nothing for years. They've just treated water. That's, that's exactly right. Um, we did a good piece of research with Henry who has, uh, a little data lake that we were, um, very, um, pleased to be able to collaborate with them.

And I wonder if you might do the top line for that, Michelle. I love, Um, so I think, you know, in terms of that, uh, we see that in terms of that sole trader, um, database that they have access to, there are people on lower incomes, then we would ordinarily see perhaps from wage and salary earners or more people perhaps on low incomes. I think the con the factor that's quite, um, difficult to tease RT is that many are in a hybrid situation as you described.

Yes. So they might be earning wages and salaries from, um, some type of employment, but they're also doing other work on the side. So, um, in this analysis that we're doing, um, using the data, um, in the IDI, we can see that different income streams that people have and are able to then look at the distribution of, of income across the different groups.

So we will have more to, to say on that once we finish that analysis. Mm. What's been really interesting to me as I've, um, held disposition is how good are we know about some of these cohorts? Yeah. Um, you think there's lots of data out there and there is kind of, but it's not joined up. And so commissions like mine, I think one of our core jobs is to go digging in a particular cohort, see what we can find out.

Thank you. Subary, Maureen, um, may not even be able to answer this one either, but, um, do you know geographically, um, where some of those pressures, uh, felt more than others? For older people? Yes. Um, for the savings that, that we've just been talking about. So I don't know whether we've got two geographical data. Do we?

I don't, we haven't done it in that. No, we don't. But you can assume that generally speaking, most of the regions, the, um, average wage is a little lower than the major centers. So those things tend to correlate. But do we have anything specific about that? No. Okay. Not counting Queenstown possibly, although then you've got a whole bunch of bank workers always it out.

The, the median average salary there is $10,000 lower than the national average. There's a rule almost. Yeah. Um, you question Helen, I have to pick, dammit. I'm gonna ask you about housing and long-term rental, which I see has got lots of potential to allow older people to move into a situation where they release their capital, but also they're not isolated to the own age group in a, um, in a village.

A village, even though lots of people couldn't afford it anyway. But it means there's, there's multi-generational communication because they'll be often in a, um, an apartment, um, block that they share. Can you tell me, have you had any look at the potential of long-term rental as a solution to some of these issues?

Uh, not specifically. Although we have looked at the housing market overall, and there's a lot of opportunity for considered government intervention here. I think, again, these are long term tell infrastructure issues, right? Um, and the, it's a little bit like thinking about retirement income policy. The worst that can happen is a whole bunch of stop start, stop start projects.

The idea that there might be a long term strategy around housing would be a very good thing, which would absolutely include different kinds of housing and different kinds of models. You've got. Um, I mean, you've got Sam Stubbs in the private sector doing build to rent, um, at the moment, which is a great initiative. You've got the publicly funded, uh, um, project out in Cannons Creek, just open this week specifically for multi-generational Pacific if families, there's many ways you can do it, but it needs a plan.

Yeah. Any question, Warren? Um, we booked in your presentation about the roadmap and cross party collaboration going forward. Really easy to say. I know, I know. Um, but, But having said it, maybe, um, you thought about what some of those policy might be, perhaps that you, you could share with us.

I think, well, I think the only thing to hold onto of course is that change is inevitable. And so if you are doing your job as leaders of this country, then you presumably want to make sure that change, um, has, has as little negative impact as possible and as strong a positive impact as possible.

So if, if this change is coming because of the demographic changes in this country, we're just gonna have to be, then how you look at the retirement system becomes, has to be become quite strategic. Again, as I said, if you're going fiddle with the NZ super, which is a common kind of reflex action, usually is said by, um, you know, uh, well-paid men put the age up, um, doesn't, doesn't have any kind of thinking around what else do you need to do and what will be the other impacts of that.

For instance, the rise in be, uh, additional benefits. Mm-hmm. Um, the rise of the, uh, needs, the need for the unemployment benefit, um, all that stuff, you know, it's, it's a system. It has to be thought of that way. Question Willie, Um, I'm just wondering in terms of, uh, iwi leaders and, uh, what's happening with regards to that broader Maori strategy, how, um, how you dealing with that?

Is there a, a strategy around that? 'cause they've, they've always been pushing these lines in terms of, um, you know, relationships with our elders and the different needs particularly that Maori have. Um, how, how have you managed that? Not well, to be fair. Um, we've kind of run outta runway and we leaders has been incredibly busy, as you know.

Yeah. But it is absolutely a list of things that need to be done in terms of the commission, and its, and its wider engagement because the issues around older Maori are, are, you know, completely different to, to others. Um, and I've not yet built them into a wider strategy in, in an effective way. Oh, you've relationships.

So even with, um, Maori NGOs, there's, uh, you're iwi leaders is one thing, but a lot of ma number of Maori NGOs too, so that's gonna be part of the plan coming up. Yeah. And in the retirement saving space, there's some room to move here. I mean, we look at the awa scheme of La Tahu as a really good model, right. And there are other iwis developing their own schemes, which is also great.

Um, if they're all done, um, um, in a, as they will be in, in a long term strategic way, there'll be a really useful addition. Thank You, John. I've got another, another question, Jane, um, you, I'll, I'll come to you next. Um, the looking, looking at this, um, your report, you're saying that cities are more likely in districts to have lower populations going forwards.

We obviously have a very large land mass in New Zealand. Uh, we have a relatively low population base, but a very large land mass. What do we need to be thinking about in terms of ensuring that people across regional New Zealand who are likely to have a higher than average population race? If I, if I understand your data correctly, and it comes all sort, all sorts of other additional towns around transport access to healthcare, et cetera, et cetera, that's, that's often more challenging in rural and regional New Zealand.

What, what do we need to be thinking about there? You think? Well, Well, this is why these are wicked problems, right? Because it's all interconnected. Mm-hmm. So if you are going to have a, um, you know, a, a mythical happy and healthy pensioner, they will have sufficient income. Um, they will have housing security, they will be mobile, and if they're not mobile, they will have good health options.

Um, having access to good public transport is, um, particularly important, uh, in the cities. It's a fairly difficult problem in the regions, but those things, again, are all interconnected as, as you well know. Um, so I think when you are dealing with, um, it, when you are thinking with the lens of older New Zealanders, you have to look at it through a number of these different, uh, provision lens.

And it's not, it's not as if it's all about older New Zealanders taking from the system. You know, we have to also think about what they contribute to wider New Zealand. They're taxpayers for a start. Um, they're a significant part of our voluntary, of our voluntary workforce. Um, they're significant. They have significant caregiving responsibilities these days.

So it's not like it's a one way street. Um, and so I think the job of the commission is to, is to paint a picture that's just somewhat more rounded than, um, what you often see In any sort of specific things. Because I mean, public transport's a classic one. Public transport isn't gonna happen in, in rural New Zealand.

Uh, not with current technology. Like sort of any, any, anything, you know, specifically anything about rural? I don't, I don't know. We haven't looked at that in depth, have we? Um, but you, you know, it depends if, if the country's feeling really wealthy at one day you'd be looking at subsidized taxi services, wouldn't you? But it's not feeling very wealthy at the moment, so it's probably not an option.

Um, you might be thinking about community-based initiatives, um, groundswell stuff. You know, you might be thinking that, uh, and this is more local body than national, I think, but you might be thinking that in a smaller town, um, there could be, um, a, a, a voluntary carpool system for older people.

Um, a kind of nonprofit driving Miss Daisy, if you're like, I'm not sure. But those kind of solutions will probably come from the ground up. I think from the top down would be my theory. Okay. The communities know its own needs better. Mm-hmm. That's an important point. Uh, Lawrence, thank You. I've got, I've got three questions because I think just on your comment, one of the things that I remember one of the Chinese supermarkets in Auckland did was that they will have a van, they will go around and pick up all of the seniors in the neighborhood

and make them do a group shop, and there's a 10% discount if you're over the age. That's a very canny supermarket operator. Ah, I know. I thought that was a great idea. But you are absolutely right in terms of the communities knows best. Yeah. I've got two related questions. They're kind of looking at the opposite end. On one hand, when you're looking at income, are you also looking at family trust and income gained through family trusts?

Um, we are seeing, we are looking, we were talking about that yesterday. We weren't, we? Yeah. What do we know? Not much. Yeah. So again, I mean, it's, it's, it's an ongoing project at the moment to understand this. We know that, um, there, there will be income flows from those trusts. We also know that, um, when we look at the asset base of people we need to take into account those trusts as well. Um, but yeah, it's, it's part of our ongoing work.

And on the other end, you talk about currently the 13%, and I think previously reported that we'll be looking at 40% renters by 2048. What about homelessness? Do you know, have any data on homelessness? Um, we would only be looking at older New Zealanders for a start. Not just homeless per se.

No, no, no. I see homelessness with 65 plus. Yeah. I don't know, but I can't imagine it's, it's not rising, like homelessness in general is rising. And final question, uh, everything you've mentioned is very much on the, from a basic needs perspective, housing, health, transport, et cetera.

But we all know that enjoyment of life doesn't also stop at 65. Mm-hmm. Are you also looking at other things like what activities or opportunities for continued learning or et cetera that's available for people over that age of 65 as well? Yeah, I think that's more covered by the office for seniors, better later life strategy.

Um, I look at it in kind of the good old fashioned Maslow's hierarchy of needs perspective. And you would, I would like to have thought that by the time you hit 65, you'd be heading out the top Yeah. To those, um, enjoyment of life and wellbeing pieces. But we are not, which is why we are so interested in housing transport in particular, because these are basic things Yeah.

That are currently, uh, under threat and will continue to be, uh, under threat in the future as far as we can see, unless there's a good systemic policy program in place. Thank you. Um, referring to your idea of the Maslow's, um, hierarchy of needs, would there be a use for, uh, greater education initiative to get more people understanding Maslow's hierarchy of needs and, and understanding that KiwiSaver is something that no matter what they should really put aside?

I think it's, um, I think a lot of people will, will relegate that to when they're older or when they're, when they have more money, um, maybe an education drive that helps people understand the value of it at an earlier age. Because, um, it, it's hard, and I know, I know people who have surplus money from when they're younger and people who don't, but the decision is still a decision that all people can make, but the education part of it, so that they want to

That's exactly right. Um, the government, uh, this year announced compulsory financial education in schools, which is a major breakthrough. We've been working really at the commission, having been working really closely with the Ministry of Education, um, and the current, uh, fin need in schools providers. There is financial education taught in schools. It's just really haphazard.

Um, so we, we've developed a model that can be delivered into schools next year, just not quite ready to sign off, but nearly there. Um, it'll be taught through the maths and the social sciences curriculum, which means it gets a hundred percent coverage across schools in Kura. Um, and that is a major step forward. Uh, the next thing, of course, is, is the ongoing, um, marketing and conversation around KiwiSaver as a bedrock part of the system.

Um, so it becomes really important at key milestones. You know, the day you get your first pay package, if you can put aside 10%, you should, if you're paying off a student loan and you're living in New Zealand, put it in your Kiwi Saver and worry about your student loan a little bit later on. You know, in small, in small chunks. There's lots of ways you can do it, but the, the, we need employers to help with this.

Our own sorted website has a lot of information around this. Um, and, and we are, uh, marketing on an ongoing basis, um, for how to increase your own financial literacy. You know, uh, it's, uh, it's, it's a lifelong journey for most, and you get people, it's a, it's, a lot of this is around behavioral economics.

You get people at the time that they're seeking the information, you can't exactly push it onto them. So it's, you know, it is carefully done, but the schools, the school's process is, is brilliant. And Michelle, If I can just add to that, I think, um, what's been remarkable about Kiwi Save is how it has worked so well in terms of engaging people through, you know, the, the use of defaults and als.

I mean, we have, for young New Zealanders, almost 97, 90 8% of the population is at, or, you know, they're in KiwiSaver, they're members. And when you look at wage and salary earners, about 90% are actively contributing. So it's been a remarkably successful scheme for, you know, specific segments of the population, in particular wage and salary earners.

So I think that those, you know, that's, that's the good news of the system. It's worked really well. Um, and there are a lot of people who are engaged in KiwiSaver. Yeah. But we're also seeing, um, for example, people are withdrawing from KiwiSaver more and more due to financial hardship. We're seeing an increase of 56.6% this year, I think reported in July of, you know, people withdrawing.

I guess the question there is everything you've said is on the basis of there is a concern, but it's on the basis of steady as we go. What happens in the event there is a either a, a manmade, you know, a human made economic shock or a natural disaster or something else like a pandemic happening.

Do you do any modeling on what happens during a period of shock and the long and the impact that will have, These are what we call life shocks. Yeah. Um, and they're often a bit more personal than that. They're about job losses, divorce, they're about a health crisis. They're about a divorce, about a business failure, um, around scams increasingly these days.

So the people's resilience to respond to life shocks is really important. Uh, we have a little bit of data on this, but what we would say is it continues to be part of the education system that providers, Kiwi safer providers and banks and the financial sector need to really focus on, um, that the community mentors and budget advisors out there who do, who do great work, um, who are there to help pick up the pieces and start people again.

Um, but You want, might I just, um, we, we outta time, I'll just one last question and then we have to, um, um, close, close it, uh, for this point at least is the, the West as a whole is facing an aging population and challenge. And I, I see here that, uh, there's 75% chance that by the mid sixties, sorry, by the mid 2060s, um, more than half New Zealand population will be over the age of 45.

What do, what does that mean in the context of, of New Zealand in the context of sort of global population aging, um, demographic demography? I can only really talk about the New Zealand context. Um, and again, I refer you to, refer you to this report. This is the changing democracy of this country is, is staring us in the face.

Um, it's happening around us. We do have time. We are aging a little more slowly than some of our, um, other country counterparts, but this is why we've said very strongly we need a 10 year plan around this, because there will be fiscal pressures, but there will also be social pressures and other pressures. There will be some opportunities here.

Um, and if we think about it strategically, um, there is an opportunity for this country to, to work together and figure out what to do. There will be a little bit of pain here, but there'll be some adjustments here. And what we are all looking for, I think is an equitable and fair system that the country can afford. That would be the goal.

Well, thank you very much, uh, both of you for coming in this, um, this morning and, and Toni, um, fascinat discussion. Appreciate it. Thank you So much. Thank you for your time. Appreciate your time. Merry Christmas. Happy New Year. Merry Christmas. Do you have a copy that Sure, yeah. You can have that one that isn't Michelle's notes or sprawled in it.

Thank you so much. You very well. You wanted to email? I don't think I've got a copy. Yeah, I'd like a copy email. I don't think I've got one. Oh, good morning, Paul. Welcome. Good Morning, Joseph. On mute again. Yeah, yeah, I know, I know. It's, it's funny how that still continues to be a thing for all of us and me included.

Look, uh, welcome. Uh, thank you very much for, uh, coming on this morning, um, as we head towards the, uh, the final close of parliament for this year. But, uh, just to give us a bit of a briefing on, on what you understand around, uh, demography. Um, and, uh, look, we, we have 15 or so minutes with you this morning. Um, what I propose, if you could, um, give us some sort of opening, um, comments and what you think is an interesting, and then maybe if we have time for some questions from members.

Yes. Um, good morning everybody. And, um, probably some of you don't, um, are aware that, uh, I headed up a, a major research program which ended a couple of years ago called the Diversity Dividend. And part of what I'm really interested in is, uh, demographic transition.

Although I think given the size of what's happening and how significant it is, it's probably more accurate to call it a demographic, uh, transformation. And we, we began to see major demographic change as we emerge from the global financial crisis 2012.

And that we saw particularly in three things, the increasing number of people reaching the age of 65, um, growing immigration as a contribution to our population. And quite soon afterwards, a very sharp fall in our fertility. So the, the key components are a rapid aging, a fertility decline, which is 25% below replacement levels now, and will drop further, um, the increasing concentration of the population in the top half of the north island.

And of course the impacts of of immigration. Joseph, I just wouldn't mind focusing on aging for a second because I think it illustrates what we, what we're talking about. And it follows Jane's, uh, presentation. We talk about numerical aging. So if we go back to when I was a boy, uh, growing up in the 1960s in Hawke's Bay, we had about 200,000 people over the age of 65.

We've currently got 900,000 and mid-century will hit a peak of 1.5 million. So we also talk about structural aging, which is the proportion of the over 60 fives as a proportion of the total population. So again, uh, going back to my boyhood 1960, it was 8%.

It's currently 18%, and mid-century 25% of all of us will be aged over 65. So you can see the size of the, the transformation, and we're gonna hit tipping points quite soon. So we talk about an old dominant population when the over 60 fives, uh, 20% or more of the population that will occur 18% at the moment, that will occur within the next decade.

Uh, but we also talk about hyper aging, which is where 30% or more of a community or a country are aged over 65, we won't get there. Jane is absolutely right. We're we are, we are gonna be slower to reach that hyper aging number, uh, compared to some other countries. But we've already got significant parts of the country which are hyper age.

So the TMS coreman is already 34% aged over 65. Um, why does it matter? Well, we talk about entry and exit ratios, and the larger large baby boom population, which will exit the workforce over the coming decades, is not going to be replaced by the entry, um, numbers because of lower fertility.

So we, we've got an imbalance and we should anticipate worker shortages.
